Deep low pressure over Pennsylvania brought copious amounts of precipitation to western New York. West of the Genesee River the precipitation fell mainly as heavy, wet snow with eight to twelve inches reported across much of the area. Locally heavier amounts of up to two feet were reported along the ridges southeast of Lake Erie. Numerous accidents were blamed on the spring snowstorm. Tens of thousands were without power as the heavy snow downed trees, limbs and power lines. In Chautauqua county, the weight of the snow damaged vineyards, breaking vine posts and roots. In Stockton, the weight of the snow caused a barn to collapse, killing 16 head of cattle. In Niagara Falls, a large inflated golf dome collapsed under the weight of the snow. Two workers were injured. They had been inside checking for snow and water damage when the dome collapsed.

A storm system moved from the Ohio Valley on Saturday, April 23rd northeast across southern Quebec and northern New York on Sunday, April 24th. Rain spread across the area with total rainfall across Essex county of up to 2 inches. Rivers became swollen and eventually flooded. Some of the rivers that flooded were the Ausable, Bouquet and Putnam Creek. The river gauge on the Ausable River crested at 9.5 feet. Portions of local roads across the county were closed, especially in the towns of Ausable Forks, Jay and Keene. Portions of Rte 73 and county routes 8 and 9 were closed. Water flowed across portions of route 9N near New Russia. The more significant flooding occurred across the east half of the county.

A storm system in the upper Great Lakes region on Tuesday, April 26th moved east across southern Quebec during Wednesday, April 27th. Associated rainfall with this system was 1/2 to 1 inch with rises on most rivers. Minor flooding of fields and low lying areas occurred along the Ausable River during the night of April 27 into the early morning of April 28th. The river gauge on the Ausable River crested at 7.1 feet.
An ice jam on the Great Chazy River at Perry Mills, NY resulted in river levels exceeding flood stage. Minor flooding of low lying areas resulted.

A storm system moved from Pennsylvania on April 2nd into New York State on April 3rd. Warmer air, rainfall and melting snow resulted in rising river levels. Minor flooding resulted along stretches of the Ausable River, with most of the flooding minor and impacting fields and low lying areas especially in eastern Essex county.

Tonawanda Creek at Batavia was above its 8' flood stage for 5 hours and crested at 9.10 feet and at Rapids was above its 12' flood stage for 48 hours and crested at 13.78 feet. The Black River at Watertown was above its 10' flood stage for 53 hours and crested at 10.93 feet. The Genesee River at Avon was  above its 33' flood stage for 21 hours and crested at 33.46 feet.

A storm system moved from the Ohio Valley on Saturday, April 23rd northeast across southern Quebec and northern New York on Sunday, April 24th. Rain spread across the area with total rainfall across Essex county of up to 2 inches. Rivers became swollen and eventually flooded. Some of the rivers that flooded were the Ausable, Bouquet and Putnam Creek. The river gauge on the Ausable River crested at 9.5 feet. Portions of local roads across the county were closed, especially in the towns of Ausable Forks, Jay and Keene. Portions of Rte 73 and county routes 8 and 9 were closed. Water flowed across portions of route 9N near New Russia. The more significant flooding occurred across the east half of the county.

A storm system moved from the Ohio Valley on Saturday, April 23rd northeast across southern Quebec and northern New York on Sunday, April 24th. Rain spread across the area with total rainfall across Clinton county of 1 to 11/2 inches. Rivers became swollen across the area. Minor flooding occurred along the Great Chazy River near Champlain, NY.

A storm system in the upper Great Lakes region on Tuesday, April 26th moved east across southern Quebec during Wednesday, April 27th. Associated rainfall with this system was 1/2 to 1 inch with rises on most rivers. Minor flooding of fields and low lying areas occurred along the Ausable River during the night of April 27 into the early morning of April 28th. The river gauge on the Ausable River crested at 7.1 feet.

A slow moving storm from the Ohio Valley brought 2 to 4 inches of rain on April 2nd and 3rd.  Before this storm, the rivers had high flows due to a previous rainstorm March 28th and snowmelt.  By the time the river fell below flood stage most of the snow had melted.  The Delaware River at Barryville rose to its flood stage of 17 feet the evening of the 2nd, crested at an estimated 24.80 feet at 9:00 AM on the 3rd, then fell back below flood stage the morning of the 4th.  This was the second highest flood of record and the highest in almost 50 years.

A slow moving storm from the Ohio Valley brought 2 to 4 inches of rain on April 2nd and 3rd.  Before this storm, the rivers had high flows due to a previous rainstorm March 28th and snowmelt.  By the time the river fell below flood stage most of the snow had melted.  The Delaware River at Callicoon rose to its flood stage of 12 feet the evening of the 2nd, crested at 17.97 feet at 8:00 AM on the 3rd, then fell back below flood stage the morning of the 4th.  This was the flood of record.  The next highest crest was 17.33 feet from the remnants of hurricane Ivan the previous September.

A slow moving storm from the Ohio Valley brought 1 to 4 inches of rain on April 2nd and 3rd.  Before this storm, the rivers and streams had high flows due to a previous rainstorm March 28th and snowmelt.  There was some road closures and flooded basements.  A few streams and creeks came out of their banks.

Deep low pressure over Pennsylvania brought copious amounts of precipitation to western and central New York...falling mainly as rain across much of the area. Rainfalls totals generally ranged from two to three inches. The rain, combined with snowmelt, produced flooding. Roads were closed in Gorham, Scottsville, Newark, Williamson, Sodus and Lowville. Basement flooding was reported in Gorham and Rochester. Evacuations were necessary in some locations in Wayne county. The heavy rains produced slick roads that were blamed for numerous automobile accidents in Monroe and Wayne counties. In Murray, Orleans county, a 50-foot section of the south bank of the Erie Canal collapsed. In Ontario county, a Lyons man was treated for hypothermia after being rescued from flood waters. The man drove his ATV into 8-10" of water, however the swift current rolled the four-wheeler over and the rider was swept away. Six area creeks and rivers reached flood stage. Oatka Creek at Garbutt was above its 6' flood stage for 48 hours and crested at 7.03 feet. Black Creek at Churchville was above its 6' flood stage for 51 hours and crested at 7.76 feet. Tonawanda Creek at Batavia was above its 8' flood stage for 5 hours and crested at 9.10 feet and at Rapids was above its 12' flood stage for 48 hours and crested at 13.78 feet. The Black River at Watertown was above its 10' flood stage for 53 hours and crested at 10.93 feet. The Genesee River at Avon was  above its 33' flood stage for 21 hours and crested at 33.46 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northeast winds produced by a coastal storm southeast of Long Island combined with high astronomical tides caused moderate coastal  flooding, mainly along the back bay shores of Nassau and Suffolk Counties.  Northeast winds averaged between 20 and 25 mph with gusts from 35 to 45 mph.In Freeport, up to 4 feet of water accumulated in the streets.  Similarly, moderate street flooding also occurred in Lindenhurst and Amityville.  In Southampton, up to 4 feet of water accumulated along Tiana Beach and Dune Road along Shinnecock Bay, where 1 family evacuated their home and 1 police car was submerged.

Low pressure, the remains of Hurricane Arlene, moved northeast across the Great Lakes. The bands of showers and thunderstorms produced damage across parts of the Niagara Frontier and Finger Lakes. Heavy rains produced flash flooding in Livingston county--an area which received heavy rains on several consecutive days. Several streets were impassable from runoff debris. The strong winds downed trees and limbs in Le Roy, Stafford, Mendon, Livonia, Lockport, Kendall, and Clarkson. Power outages were scattered throughout the area.

Thunderstorms rolled across the area during the evening and early overnight hours. The storms produced up to five inches of rain in some areas. Centerline, Maxon and Humphrey Roads in Wyoming County were closed because of damage and erosion of their shoulders. In Aurora, Erie County, a garage floor washed away resulting in damage to a motorcycle and SUV. In Cheektowaga, parts of the Scajaquada Expressway were closed when flood waters covered the road. Lightning struck the Eden Town Hall. Electronic equipment, including the main Police radio counsel, was damaged throughout the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a southward moving cold front on the afternoon of June 22nd. Some of the storms became severe as they headed in a general southward direction. They produced both large hail and very strong wind gusts.

Slow moving thunderstorms during the late afternoon hours produced torrential rains of three to four inches across parts of the Genesee Valley and Finger Lakes. In South Bristol, Ontario county, the intersection of Hicks and Seneca Point Roads was closed until crews could clear mud and debris carried by flood waters. In Centerville, Allegany county, a bridge was washed out on Pratt Road.

A weak cold front crossed the area and was accompanied by damaging thunderstorms. The winds destroyed a barn in Bolivar. In Wellsville, the winds broke a housing unit on top of the Elementary School, breaking a natural gas line and filling parts of the school with gas and a strong odor. The school was evacuated and no injuries were reported. The strong winds downed trees in Genesee, Scio and Wellsville.

Low pressure moved slowly across western Quebec Wednesday, June 15th. The associated frontal system was lined up north to south across eastern New York State as it moved slowly east. Showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain impacted Essex county. Flooding of roads, culverts and basements was reported in Ticonderoga, NY. Heavy rain to a lesser extent also fell across Franklin and Clinton counties of NY.

A low pressure system moved across southern Canada during Monday, June 6th. The associated warm front moved across the area with thunderstorms developing in the warm and humid air mass. A few storms were severe in Essex county, with nickle size hail reported in Elizabethtown.

A warm and very humid airmass was in place across northern New York. Thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a  cold front as it moved southeast across northern New York during the afternoon. Thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall impacted the town of Altona with streams and culverts running full and water ponding on roads.
A warm and very humid airmass was in place across northern New York. Thunderstorms developed across St Lawrence county with nickle size hail reported in Oswegatchie.
An isolated severe thunderstorm developed along a cold front pushing through the area. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines in Newark.

Outflow boundaries cross the Finger Lakes and thunderstorms develop and intensified as they moved across the north country. The thunderstorm winds downed trees and power lines. In Adams, a home suffered damage when a tree fell onto its roof. Another home in Adams had its porch roof blown off.

An upper level low pressure system moved slowly across southeast Ontario province. The associated surface low pressure system moved slowly across the province of Quebec, as the frontal system moved slowly across eastern New York state. Thunderstorms with very heavy rainfall moved slowly south to north along the front through Essex county. The eastern portions of the county were impacted the most. Numerous roads, including portions of Rte 9,  were closed. Especially hard hit were the towns of Lewis, Willsboro, Westport, Crown Point, Moriah, Ticonderoga and Elizabethtown. A state of Emergency was declared by the county Emergency Manager. Putnam Creek and the Bouquet River exceeded their banks in places.

Lightning started a fire on the second floor of a garage on Cayuga Street.  The fire in the garage was quickly extinguished.  The lightning also shorted out the kitchen wiring and telephones in the nearby house.

Thunderstorm winds did extensive damage in a small area on Oak Hill Road in Moravia.  The damage was thought to be due to a microburst.  The winds took the roof off a large barn extension, collapsed a 70 foot tall grain elevator, and downed trees.  Other trees were downed on Rockefeller Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front. The thunderstorms produced measured winds gusting to near 60 mph which downed trees and power lines. Trees reportedly fell on houses in Avon, Gowanda and Naples. Fallen trees also damaged automobiles in Arcade, Lily Dale, Salamanca and Honeoye Falls. At Alfred State College in Allegany county, an estimated $100,000 damage occurred as the storms destroyed a dugout and storage shed, damaged athletic fields and other college properties. On Chautauqua Lake, a marina suffered substantial damage to its seawall and dockside refueling area. Also on the lake, several boats broke loose from their moorings. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers and thunderstorms intensified as it moved south.  Heavier showers produced rainfall rates between 1 and 2 inches per hour.  This resulted in widespread street flooding across central and southern Nassau County.  Here are selected storm total rainfall amounts in inches:Oceanside - 2.39.Lido Beach - 2.30.Garden City Park - 2.25.Merrick - 1.16.Spotters from Lido Beach and Levittown reported street flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary from west to east across central New York State into southern New England moved slowly north and focused thunderstorms that moved slowly across Essex county of New York. Very heavy rainfall accompanied these storms, and the eastern portion of the county sustained the most widespread damage with roads closed and power outages reported. The county Emergency Manger declared a state of emergency in this area especially in the towns of Crown Point and Moriah. Numerous roads were washed out in these towns, and all roads and bridges were closed to all traffic in those towns.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees.

Thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees and wires.  Towns affected were Danby, Ulysses, Ithaca, and Dryden.  In the village of Trumansburg, a tree took down 5 utility poles and a couple of transformers.  A tree in DeWitt Park in Ithaca fell on a pavilion.  A different tree in Ithaca fell on a car.  In Dryden, a tree fell on a convertible.

A vertically stacked cold-core cyclone was in the vicinity of eastern New York State during late June 16 and on June 17.  The system spawned widespread heavy showers andthunderstorms.  Rainfall was mainly 1 to 3 inches through the Hudson-Champlain Valleycorridor.  This was enough to trigger more flash flooding on ground previously soaked byheavy rainfall.  With the exception of a washout on Route 9 along the western shore of LakeGeorge, there was no major damage from the flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State Route 9N from Hague to Bolton Landing shut down due to a washouton Tongue Mountain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front lay through the St Lawrence valley on June 26.  The air massover eastern New York was becoming more unstable.  An isolated severe thunderstormoccurred early in the afternoon over eastern Greene County.  Damaging wind gustsuprooted trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The westerward extension of the Bermuda High Pressure poked into eastern New York.  Theair mass over the region was conditionally unstable.  Isolated showers and thunderstormswith heavy rainfall occurred.  One storm caused flash flooding during trhe afternoon and evening near North Creek in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road washouts on River Road and Cobble Creek Road.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees and wires.
Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front extended from northern Maine to extreme northern New York and then into Pennsylvania early on Friday, July 22. The front helped trigger thunderstorms as it moved across northern NY by early afternoon. Thunderstorms developed along a boundary in northeast NY in the late morning and they quickly intensified into severe thunderstorms with strong winds and large hail as they moved east and southeast across the area. In the town of Port Kent, NY, power lines, several trees and numerous tree limbs were blown down. Winds were estimated to be 60 mph (52 knots). Hail up to 3/4 inch in diameter also fell in Port Kent. In addition, lightning struck and damaged a house in Port Kent.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed many trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a highly tropical airmass on July 18. Rainfall rates in thunderstorms reached as high as 3 inches per hour. This caused severe flash flooding in several areas of Southeast New York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extremely heavy rainfall rates caused flash flooding to occur on I-287. The highway was covered with several feet of water and mud in spots. Several motorists were rescued as the rapidly rising water swamped their cars during rush hour. A police inspector was noted as saying that there were manhole covers floating down streets with several cars submerged. Water was seen cascading down several levels of a parking garage like a waterfall.

Lightning struck a home in Livonia and traveled through the wiring in the house. The siding on the house was scorched and most of the electrical appliances were burned out. Before sunrise on May 5, high pressure crested over eastern New York and adjacent westernNew England With light wind and clear sky, the air at the earth's surface cooled to below freezingacross most of eastern New York. There were numerous reports of minimum temperatures at orbelow 30 degrees. No major agricultural damage was reported to the National Weather Servicefrom this frost.

Chilly air lingered another day in a portion of eastern New York as a ridge of high pressurecentered over Nova Scotia ridged southwestward across New England into northeastern NewYork during the early morning hours of May 6.  Subfreezing air was not as widespread as itwas on May 5, however, a few locations had readings below freezing in southern WarrenCounty.  Glens Falls had the lowest reported temperature, 29 degrees.  Once again, no seriousagricultural damage was reported to the National Weather Service from this frost.

Thunderstorms developed in a unseasonably hot and humid airmass during the late afternoon and early evening hours. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in a unseasonably hot and humid airmass during the late afternoon and early evening hours. The storms downed trees and power lines in Rochester, Lockport, Evans, Batavia, Orchard Park and Spencerport. A house chimney was damaged by the downburst winds in Rochester. In Chili a woman was slightly injured while talking on the telephone when lightning traveled through the home's telephone line. Also in Chili, a house fire on Chili Avenue Extension was blamed on a lightning strike. The heavy rains that accompanied the storms resulted in flash flooding in parts of Lewis and Monroe counties. In the Rochester metro area, there were numerous reports of flooded roads and basements. In Turin, the flood waters washed out a portion of Fish Creek Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak and short-lived F1 tornado touched down around 945 pm EDT in Glen Cove. The tornado formed in a thunderstorm that formed on an outflow boundary produced by a line of thunderstorms over Long Island Sound. The tornado moved from northwest to southeast and was estimated to be on the ground for only a minute or two. However, it caused considerable damage to many of the large trees in the area. Many large trees in the neighborhood to the south of the Welwyn Preserve came down and did considerable damage to homes and property. Damage was reported as far southeast as North Shore University Hospital. There were no known injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 12, severe thunderstorms formed over parts of the Mohawk Valley and from the southern Capital District southward into parts of the mid Hudson Valley.  These thunderstorms were associated with strong winds in the upper levels of the atmosphere on the western side of a nearly stationary upper level low pressure over Nova Scotia.  The air aloft was quite cool causing instability during the afternoon hours.  A thunderstorm produced three quarter inch hail at Selkirk, in Albany County.  Strong wind gusts blew down trees and large tree limbs across portions of Greene, Columbia and Fulton counties.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front. The thunderstorms produced measured winds gusting to near 60 mph which downed trees and power lines. Trees reportedly fell on houses in Avon, Gowanda and Naples. Fallen trees also damaged automobiles in Arcade, Lily Dale, Salamanca and Honeoye Falls. At Alfred State College in Allegany county, an estimated $100,000 damage occurred as the storms destroyed a dugout and storage shed, damaged athletic fields and other college properties. On Chautauqua Lake, a marina suffered substantial damage to its seawall and dockside refueling area. Also on the lake, several boats broke loose from their moorings. Utility companies reported that at the peak of the storm nearly 30,000 customers were without power.  A 14-year old girl was critically injured at the Chautauqua Institution when a 4-foot diameter trees was felled by the thunderstorm winds and crushed her. A 50-year old man was injured by lightning in Webster. Lightning struck a tree and traveled through the electrical wiring in his home. Lightning also damaged the chimney of the Stanley Fire Department in Ontario county. Structure fires, reportedly started by lightning, occurred in Medina, Clarendon, Ridgeway and Shelby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front extended from northern Maine to extreme northern New York and then into Pennsylvania early on Friday, July 22. The front helped trigger thunderstorms as it moved across northern NY by early afternoon, and across Vermont during the afternoon. Thunderstorms developed along a boundary in northeast NY in the late morning and they quickly intensified into severe thunderstorms with strong winds and large hail as they moved east and southeast across the area. Hail up to 1 inch in diameter was reported in Plattsburgh, NY while strong wind gusts took down tree limbs in Peru, NY.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 8, a stationary front lay east to west across eastern New York and central New England.The front was in the vicinity of the Mohawk valley, the Capital District of New York, andthe Berkshires.  At upper levels, a weak disturbance moved across Quebec Province,Canada.  The southern periphery of this feature affected eastern New York and westernNew England during the afternoon.  The air mass along, and to the south of the stationaryfront was unstable enough to trigger thunderstorms.  Damaging thunderstorm wind gustsoccurred at several locations in eastern New York.  They were concentrated in the LakeGeorge region and the lower Catskill Mountain area.  But trees were also blown down in thehilly areas east of the Capital District.  No hail or flash flooding occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low pressure moved eastward across Quebec Province on June 10.  A coolfront moved slowly southward to near the St Lawrence Valley where it stalled.  The air massover eastern New York was humid and unstable.  Random heavy thunderstorms formed duringthe afternoon.   Over two inches of rain fell in a short time from one thunderstorm in the vicinity ofRound Lake.  Water inundated several roadways near Round Lake, resulting in their closure.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flash flooding.  There was a report of over2 inches of rain from an Emergency Manager.

At daybreak on July 27, a weak low pressure wave was on a slow moving cold front was over western New York State.  The air over eastern New York and western New England was warm, humid and unstable.  The low pressure and cold front moved northeastward during the day.  Severe thunderstorms formed late in the afternoon from the mid Hudson Valley northward across Columbia and Rensselaer counties.  Damaging wind gusts blew down trees in Poughkeepsie and Pawling, in Dutchess County; near West Sand Lake, in Rensselaer County and in Esopus and Port Ewen, in Ulster County.  Trees and wires were down at Claverack, in Columbia County.

Lightning started a fire at a seasonal residence on North Steuben Road.  The 20 by 30 foot home was seriously damaged and suffered a collapsed roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly across southeast Ontario province into northern New York State. The associated surface low pressure system drifted across northern New York during the morning and northwest Vermont in the afternoon. Slow moving showers and thunderstorms overnight across Clinton and Essex counties impacted areas that already experienced heavy rainfall. The eastern portions of both counties were the most impacted. Rivers continued to run bankfull, with flooding in the Essex county towns of Wadhams, Westport, Lewis, Jay, Port Henry, Moriah, Willsboro, Crown Point and Ticonderoga. Numerous roads, including portions of Rte 9,  were closed. A state of Emergency had been declared by the Essex county Emergency Manager. Putnam Creek and the Bouquet River exceeded their banks in places. The Ausable River at Ausable Forks reached 7.8 feet (flood stage is 7 feet)  shortly after 7 am on the 17th with river flooding. Flooding continued in Keeseville in Clinton county.

Thunderstorm winds downed trees and wires across the southeast quarter of the county.  Damage was widespread in Corning, South Corning, East Corning, Caton, Campbell, Addison, and Bath.  In Corning, wires came down on a car and delivery truck.  In Campbell, the wind ripped off the roofing from a house and downed a tree onto a truck, minivan, and camper.  In Corning, a tree fell onto a pickup truck crushing it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system moved slowly across southeast Ontario province into northern New York State. The associated surface low pressure system drifted across northern New York during the morning and northwest Vermont in the afternoon. Slow moving showers and thunderstorms overnight across Clinton and Essex counties impacted areas that already experienced heavy rainfall. The eastern portions of both counties were the most impacted. Rivers continued to run bankfull, with flooding in the Essex county towns of Wadhams, Westport, Lewis, Jay, Port Henry, Moriah, Willsboro, Crown Point and Ticonderoga. Numerous roads, including portions of Rte 9,  were closed. A state of Emergency had been declared by the Essex county Emergency Manager. Putnam Creek and the Bouquet River exceeded their banks in places. The Ausable River at Ausable Forks reached 7.8 feet (flood stage is 7 feet)  shortly after 7 am on the 17th with river flooding. Flooding continued in Keeseville in Clinton county.

On June 6, A stationary front was oriented north to south through central New York and curved eastward south of Long Island.  An unstable air mass was over eastern New York.An upper-level short wave trough approached from the Great Lakes.  Thunderstorms developed in lineoriented north to south across eastern New York around noon.  Several severe thunderstorms weresevere and widespread wind damage occurred, and a few reports of hail up to an inch were received.Thirty-four thousand people were without power in the northern Saratoga and southern Warren County region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down throughout the county.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees.  A tree fell on a house.

Lightning hit the Ruby Tuesday restaurant at the Arnot Mall in Big Flats.  The lightning lifted the roof off the building, shut off the electricity, and burned out the air conditioning unit.  The smoke from the unit and the power outage temporarily closed the mall.
Trees were blown down.
Trees and wires were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure, the remains of Hurricane Arlene, moved northeast across the Great Lakes. The bands of showers and thunderstorms produced damage across parts of the Niagara Frontier and Finger Lakes. Heavy rains produced flash flooding in Livingston county--an area which received heavy rains on several consecutive days. Several streets were impassable from runoff debris. The strong winds downed trees and limbs in Le Roy, Stafford, Mendon, Livonia, Lockport, Kendall, and Clarkson. Power outages were scattered throughout the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding.  Several roads were closed.  Mobile home knocked off its foundation from flowing water.  1 water rescue.  1 bridge destroyed and another bridge damaged.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees and wires in Big Flats, Horseheads, Elmira, and Southport.  A tree fell onto a house in Big Flats.  Also in Big Flats a trailer was blown off its foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, very humid air mass had been over eastern New York and adjacent western New England duringthe weekend.  On Monday, June 13, the remnant of tropical storm Arlene was over the central Great Lakesshortly after daybreak.  It moved eastward triggering a cluster of thunderstorms in the vicinity of Lake Georgeduring the late afternoon and evening.  These thunderstorms contained very heavy rainfall.  Between 6 and 7inches of rainfall occurred in about 2-hour period.  The heavy rainfall continued into the early morning of June 14.Small creeks became swollen rivers.  Some officials described the rainfall in the Bolton Landing area as a"500-year-storm".  There were several road washouts in Warren County, including the major north-souththoroughfare I-87, known as the Northway.  Severe damage to that roadway occurred near mile 67,  and the roadwas closed for several days between exits 23 and 25 (about 16 miles).  Several other roads were damagedextensively, especially those around Bolton Landing.  The damage to roads alone was in excess of 6 million dollars.At least 20 people were evacuated, and a number of homes were damaged.  Governor Pataki declared WarrenCounty a "disaster area".
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Warren County during the evening hours of June13 through the early morning hours of June 14.  The flash flooding was focusedfrom central Lake George westward.  At 630 PM EST, it was reported that waterwas flowing down Dowen Mountain, and that road culverts were running like rivers.At 730 PM EST, it was reported that Interstate I-87, the Northway, was closed dueto a mud slide between exists 23 and 25.  At 812 PM, it was reported that numerousroads were closed in the Bolton and Chestertown due to flooding.  At 1205 AM onJune 14, it was reported that Interstate 87, the Northway, was washed out at milepost67, and that vast amounts of water were flowing over the highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front lay east to west through the St Lawrence Valley at daybreak on June 29.  A weakupper-level short wave trough was moving eastward across the Great Lakes.  During the day, the front drifteda little further south into the unstable air mass over New York State.  But it stalled across the AdirondackMountains.  This boundary and the approaching upper level short wave combined to trigger severe thunderstorms.Several thunderstorms produced severe wind gusts and/or torrential downpours during the afternoon and evening.There were several reports of wind damage and flash flooding.  The flooding was most widespread from the CapitalDistrict to Lake George during the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed over the Lower Hudson Valley on the afternoon of June 29th in association with a slow moving front. The atmosphere was very moist, which allowed the thunderstorms to produce significant hourly rainfall rates of greater than 2 inches. In a matter of 2 to 4 hours, some locations in Rockland and the southern half of Westchester County picked up anywhere from 3 to 6 inches of rain. Scores of automobiles were submerged, with motorists having to be rescued by Law Enforcement officials. Train service in southern Westchester County was halted. Scores of roads were closed in both counties, causing major backups during rush hour. Many roads washed out or undermined.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall rates of over 2 inches per hour.
Thunderstorm winds downed trees and wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowfall hit parts of upstate New York on October 25th, 2005. Low pressure developed over Ohio and tracked to off the New Jersey Coast where it intensified into a major early season winter storm. Snow fell mainly over the higher elevations of central New York and the Catskills, with mainly rain in the valleys. The rain caused minor flood problems in low-lying areas near Waverly along the Susquehanna River. The hardest hits areas were from the higher terrain of southern and eastern Broome County, north and east through the higher terrain of Chenango, western Delaware, eastern Cortland, southern Madison, Otsego and southern Onondaga counties. Between 6 and 12 inches of heavy wet snow fell above 1400 feet elevation in these areas. Highest amounts were found in southern Madison County and in Chenango County at 1800 feet where more than 12 inches of wet snow fell. Between 2 and 6 inches of snow fell between 1000 and 1400 feet in these areas. The snow came down very hard from the late morning into the early and mid afternoon hours on the 25th. The snow continued heavy at times until the mid to late evening hours on the 25th. The snow rapidly accumulated on roads and caused major traffic problems along interstates 81, 88 and route 17 in southern New York and in the Binghamton area. In addition, the heavy wet snow brought down trees and power lines in these areas. Over 10,000 customers were without power in Broome and Chenango Counties alone.  Across the Central Southern Tier, and the Finger Lakes region, the valleys saw rain with the higher terrain, above 1600 feet, seeing only a few inches of snow. In the Catskills, the higher terrain above 1800 feet saw up to 6 inches of snow. The Syracuse and Utica areas saw little snowfall, with the Southern Tug Hill Plateau reporting up to a few inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down over a widespread area in Piseco.

A large ocean storm system moved north, passing east of New England on Tuesday, October 25th and Wednesday, October 26th. Rain on the 25th of October changed to snow by early afternoon in the higher terrain. This heavy wet snow was accompanied by gusty winds. With foliage still on the trees, the weight of the snow easily took many trees and tree limbs down with extensive power outages. During the evening, it was clearly evident that the impact of the storm was being felt with 3 to 6 inches of heavy wet snow. Total snow accumulations in this area were 5 to 10 inches with lesser amounts in some sheltered valleys. Specifically, Ellenburg Depot (western Clinton county) received 10 inches, while the Franklin county towns of Whippleville received 9 inches, and Tupper Lake 5 inches. In western Essex county, Lake Placid received 6 1/2 inches of heavy wet snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees down

On October 8 at daybreak, a nearly stationary cold front was over southeastern New York.  The air over the northeastern United States was very moist.  Low pressure in the vicinity of the eastern Carolina states moved slowly north northeast along the cold front.  Heavy rain fell over southeastern New York through the early morning hours of October 9.  During this period, there was over 6 inches of rainfall in  southeastern New York...mainly south and east of the Capital District.  This triggered widespread flooding and several evacuations of people from their homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wappingers Creek had a flood crest of 8.33 feet at Wappingers Falls on Oct 9, at 7:15 PM EST.
Wappingers Creek had a flood crest of 11.16 feet at Wappingers Falls, New York on Oct 15, at 4:45 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowfall hit parts of upstate New York on October 25th, 2005. Low pressure developed over Ohio and tracked to off the New Jersey Coast where it intensified into a major early season winter storm. Snow fell mainly over the higher elevations of central New York and the Catskills, with mainly rain in the valleys. The rain caused minor flood problems in low-lying areas near Waverly along the Susquehanna River. The hardest hits areas were from the higher terrain of southern and eastern Broome County, north and east through the higher terrain of Chenango, western Delaware, eastern Cortland, southern Madison, Otsego and southern Onondaga counties. Between 6 and 12 inches of heavy wet snow fell above 1400 feet elevation in these areas. Highest amounts were found in southern Madison County and in Chenango County at 1800 feet where more than 12 inches of wet snow fell. Between 2 and 6 inches of snow fell between 1000 and 1400 feet in these areas. The snow came down very hard from the late morning into the early and mid afternoon hours on the 25th. The snow continued heavy at times until the mid to late evening hours on the 25th. The snow rapidly accumulated on roads and caused major traffic problems along interstates 81, 88 and route 17 in southern New York and in the Binghamton area. In addition, the heavy wet snow brought down trees and power lines in these areas. Over 10,000 customers were without power in Broome and Chenango Counties alone.  Across the Central Southern Tier, and the Finger Lakes region, the valleys saw rain with the higher terrain, above 1600 feet, seeing only a few inches of snow. In the Catskills, the higher terrain above 1800 feet saw up to 6 inches of snow. The Syracuse and Utica areas saw little snowfall, with the Southern Tug Hill Plateau reporting up to a few inches.

The remnants of Katrina moved northeast from the Tennessee Valley on August 30th and combined with a frontal system over the Ohio Valley early on the morning of the 31st of August. This system moved northeast through the St Lawrence Valley region of New York during the 31st of August, and continued into Canada the night of the 31st. Steady rain overspread the area late on the 30th of August and was heavy on the 31st of August, supplied with the tropical moisture from Katrina. Rainfall totals across the New York county of Clinton was 3 to 4 inches while the county of Essex received 2 to 3 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked for approximately 1 mile in the town of Canisteo.  The maximum width was 200 yards but the worst damage was 75 to 100 yards wide.  The tornado started on the east side of Bush Hill just west of State Route 36 about 1 mile south of Gravel Run Road.  The tornado moved south along Route 36 and Colonel Bills Creek before moving across both prior to reaching Rock Run Road.  The majority of the damage was to large trees.  An estimated 100 to 200 trees were uprooted or snapped in half.  Significant crop damage was observed in a cornfield.  A barn had one side of its tin roof peeled off.  Other damage at the farm included a sheared electric pole, a recreational vehicle with two tree branches embedded in it, a silage wagon that was moved, electric lines ripped from the house and barn.

The northeast US was impacted by 2 weather systems on September 16th and 17th. A storm moved northeast along a frontal boundary, from the Ohio valley on the 16th and passed across northern New York the morning of the 17th. In addition, moisture from Tropical Storm Ophelia impacted the area. Steady rain with scattered thunderstorms overspread northern New York on the afternoon of the 16th into the late night hours. Specifically, across the southern half of St Lawrence county, between 3 and 5 inches of rain fell. When the entire overnight period is added into the afternoon rainfall, 6.46 inches of rain was measured in Gouverneur, NY. Flash flooding was reported with road closures in Governeur, Pierrepont, Fowler, Colton and Hermon. Flooding of cellars was reported in Canton. Elsewhere across the Adirondacks of northern NY, between 2 and 3 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the predawn hours on May 13, high pressure over southeastern Quebec Province,Canada, ridged southeastward into eastern New York and adjacent western New England.With a clear sky and a light wind, cooling at the earth's surface was optimized.  Thetemperatures dropped below freezing for several hours.  New York Dept. of Agriculture andMarkets indicated that there was extensive damage to the fruit crop but could not yetprovide a dollar value to the damage.

Severe thunderstorms developed ahead of an approaching cold front. The thunderstorms produced measured winds gusting to near 60 mph which downed trees and power lines. Trees reportedly fell on houses in Avon, Gowanda and Naples. Fallen trees also damaged automobiles in Arcade, Lily Dale, Salamanca and Honeoye Falls. At Alfred State College in Allegany county, an estimated $100,000 damage occurred as the storms destroyed a dugout and storage shed, damaged athletic fields and other college properties. On Chautauqua Lake, a marina suffered substantial damage to its seawall and dockside refueling area. Also on the lake, several boats broke loose from their moorings. Utility companies reported that at the peak of the storm nearly 30,000 customers were without power.  A 14-year old girl was critically injured at the Chautauqua Institution when a 4-foot diameter trees was felled by the thunderstorm winds and crushed her. A 50-year old man was injured by lightning in Webster. Lightning struck a tree and traveled through the electrical wiring in his home. Lightning also damaged the chimney of the Stanley Fire Department in Ontario county. Structure fires, reportedly started by lightning, occurred in Medina, Clarendon, Ridgeway and Shelby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A humid airmass was in place with dew points around 70 on the afternoon of July 17th. The remnants of tropical depression Dennis moved off the mid-Atlantic coast, with tropical air across the eastern US. Winds aloft were weak, thus thunderstorms were slow movers in our area. Thunderstorms developed in northern New York, but were more organized in St Lawrence county with very heavy rainfall. The heaviest rain fell over the west half of St Lawrence county, specifically the southwest portion of the county. A weather spotter in Gouverneur, NY reported 4.10 inches of rainfall during the afternoon. Roads were closed in and around Gouverneur, NY due to flood waters on roads up to a foot deep. Portions of routes 11 and 58 in this area were washed out.

The remnants of Tropical Storm Cindy became absorbed along a cold front and moved northeast from northern Virginia early on July 8th and reached just east of Cape Cod early on the morning of July 9th. Heavy rain fell across northern New York, with 1 to 3 inches of rain in Clinton and Franklin counties. Gusty winds with saturated soil resulted in some damage to trees, and some downed tree branches with some minor roof damage in Franklin county. Additionally, some minor road damage due to the heavy rain was reported in Franklin county.

On July 22, a cold front trailing southwestward through the St Lawrence Valley from a low pressure center off the Canadian maritime provinces moved through eastern New York during the afternoon.  It reached the Atlantic coast just before midnight.  The cold front moved through an unstable air mass and touched off thunderstorms, several of which produced damaging wind gusts in Albany, Greene and Columbia counties. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 18, a weakening low pressure that had been over the mid Atlantic region on July 17, moved across southeastern New York.  This system was associated with a tropical air mass.  The weak surface low pressure and a minor upper level short wave trough set off thunderstorms in southeastern New York.  Several thunderstorms contained very heavy rainfall which produced flash flooding.  One thunderstorm over Shandaken, Ulster County contained damaging wind gusts that blew down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road closures due to flooding.
Trees down throughout the eastern part of Montgomery County, some fell on houses.The time that this occurred is estimated.
State Route 5 and Truax Road washed out.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms formed ahead of an advancing cold front. The airmass ahead of the front was moist and unstable. As the thunderstorms moved into the Lower Hudson Valley, they became severe and knocked down several trees which caused power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines down in Scarsdale and Tarrytown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early season snowfall hit parts of upstate New York on October 25th, 2005. Low pressure developed over Ohio and tracked to off the New Jersey Coast where it intensified into a major early season winter storm. Snow fell mainly over the higher elevations of central New York and the Catskills, with mainly rain in the valleys. The rain caused minor flood problems in low-lying areas near Waverly along the Susquehanna River. The hardest hits areas were from the higher terrain of southern and eastern Broome County, north and east through the higher terrain of Chenango, western Delaware, eastern Cortland, southern Madison, Otsego and southern Onondaga counties. Between 6 and 12 inches of heavy wet snow fell above 1400 feet elevation in these areas. Highest amounts were found in southern Madison County and in Chenango County at 1800 feet where more than 12 inches of wet snow fell. Between 2 and 6 inches of snow fell between 1000 and 1400 feet in these areas. The snow came down very hard from the late morning into the early and mid afternoon hours on the 25th. The snow continued heavy at times until the mid to late evening hours on the 25th. The snow rapidly accumulated on roads and caused major traffic problems along interstates 81, 88 and route 17 in southern New York and in the Binghamton area. In addition, the heavy wet snow brought down trees and power lines in these areas. Over 10,000 customers were without power in Broome and Chenango Counties alone.  Across the Central Southern Tier, and the Finger Lakes region, the valleys saw rain with the higher terrain, above 1600 feet, seeing only a few inches of snow. In the Catskills, the higher terrain above 1800 feet saw up to 6 inches of snow. The Syracuse and Utica areas saw little snowfall, with the Southern Tug Hill Plateau reporting up to a few inches.

With wet antecedent conditions and many trees still with nearly full leaf canopies, strong northeast winds and heavy rain caused by a coastal storm downed many trees across the region.  Peak wind gusts were between 40 and 50 mph.The NYC Parks Department reported 169 downed trees in Queens, 98 downed trees in Brooklyn, 27 downed trees in Staten Island, 22 downed trees in Manhattan, and 19 downed trees in the Bronx.  The NYC Office of Emergency Management logged 335 telephone calls in response to downed trees, downed limbs, and hanging limbs across the 5 boroughs of New York City.A trained spotter in Suffolk County reported several downed tree limbs along with a utility pole snapped in half just west of the Route 25A and Edwards Avenue intersection in Baiting Hollow.

A strong west pressure gradient developed as an intensifying low pressure system moved north across Northern New England as a ridge of high pressure extended south across the Western Great Lakes.  With very wet antecedent conditions and most trees with full leaf canopies, many trees were downed in saturated ground as peak west winds gusted from 36 to 46 mph.An off duty NWS employee reported a tree down on a house in Hampton Bays.  The NYC Office of Emergency Management reported trees down at many locations throughout Staten Island, Brooklyn, and Queens.  They also reported that a window on the 17th floor at Seventh Avenue and Forty Ninth Street in Manhattan was blown out as glass shattered in the streets.  A tree fell on a moving car on Jackie Robinson Parkway in Flushing Queens.  Injuries were unknown.  A tree fell on a person in Clove Lakes Park, about 1 mile north of Todt Hill in Staten Island.  Injury was unknown.

Periods of heavy rain continued throughout the day.  Bands of torrential rain produced widespread and significant flash flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas, including streets, basements, roofs, and ceilings, as well as small creeks throughout Nassau and Suffolk Counties.  Trained spotters reported up to 6 feet of water in basements throughout Massapequa.  They reported serious flooding along Ocean Parkway in Brooklyn, where cars were submerged, and the Grand Central Parkway in Queens.  Several trees and power poles were leaning from soggy ground.  Preliminary damage cost estimates were $22 million for Long Island.

A mixture of snow, sleet and rain the morning of November 22nd changed to snow early in the the afternoon. The precipitation tapered off to snow showers at night. Generally between 2 and 4 inches of snow fell, with locally higher amounts in the Adirondack mountains.

A storm system located to the east of New England on Saturday, October 15th, moved north into New Brunswick, Ca during Sunday, October 16th. Strong winds resulted from a tight pressure gradient during the late morning and early afternoon hours of the 16th of October. Trees and power lines were blown down countywide across the northern New York counties of Clinton, Essex and Franklin. Power outages were reported across the area. Specifically, numerous trees were uprooted in Dannemora (Clinton county) and in Keene (Essex county).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved across northern portions of Ontario Province early on the 29th of November, and into Quebec Province by the afternoon of the 29th of November. Gusty south winds preceded the associated cold front during the morning and early afternoon hours of November 29th. In the Saranac Lake area of southern Franklin county New York, power lines were blown down on top of a vehicle which resulted in a brief road closure.

Gusty south winds preceded the associated cold front during the morning and early afternoon hours of November 29th. Weak low pressure over the mid Mississippi Valley on November 5, intensified while moving northeastward along a stationary front.  Around daybreak on November 6, the low was over Michigan with a warm front eastward to the Capital District and northern Berkshire County.   The low continued to intensify while moving across the central Great Lakes and into Canada.  The warm front moved northeastward into New England while a cold front sliced eastward into the Hudson Valley during the evening hours.   In the late afternoon and evening on November 6, a line of thunderstorms, some severe, formed along this cold front as it moved across the western Adirondacks and Mohawk Velley into the Hudson River Valley.   The thunderstorms weakened as they traveled into New England.  The thunderstorms did not produce much rain, less than half an inch, but in addition to some damaging wind gusts, there was some three-quarter-inch hail in the northwestern Catskills and central Mohawk Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported that trees and wires were blown down.
On November 30, the Esopus Creek at Mount Marion went above its 20-foot flood stage late in the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system over the northern Great Lakes region on the morning of November 6th moved northeast into Canada. It reached northern Quebec the late night of November 6th. The associated cold front moved west to east across the area during the late afternoon and evening of November 6th. Strong thunderstorms developed along and ahead of this front, and a few became severe as they transported strong winds aloft to the surface. Trees and power lines were blown down in De Kalb and Potsdam as these storms moved across St Lawrence county.

Lake effect snows began on Thanksgiving Day following the passage of a strong cold front. Snowfall totals reached a foot or more across the higher elevations of the western southern tier as well as a large portion of Jefferson and western Lewis counties. Specific reported snowfalls off Lake Erie included: 24" at Ellicottville, 22" at South Dayton; 16" at Perrysburg; 13" at Sinclairville; 11" at Lancaster; 10" at Warsaw and 7" at Buffalo. Off Lake Ontario, snow totals included: 24" at West Leyden; 22" at North Osceola; 16" at Watertown; and 14" at Turin and West Monroe.

The first lake effect event of the season brought over a foot of heavy, wet snow to the areas downwind of Lake Erie while areas east of Lake Ontario received seven to ten inches. Off Lake Erie, the heavy snow, falling at the rate of 2" per hour, concentrated within an eight mile wide stip from the lake shore near Silver Creek and Angola, across Hamburg, Orchard Park, East Aurora, Elma and Alden. The weight of the wet snow caused several buildings to collapse, including a large bowling in Hamburg. Specific snow totals included: 18" at Elma; 17" at Marilla; 16" at Orchard Park and Hamburg; 14" at Bennington; 13" at East Aurora; 9" at Carthage and Harrisville; 8" at Darien and 7" at Beaver Falls.

The passage of a cold front brought training thunderstorms to the county. Rainfall of up to two inches in less than an hour resulted in flash flooding in Wellsville. Basements were flooded and a portion of Route 19 was closed.

On October 27, the core of a cold air mass was over eastern Canada.  High pressure was centered over the northern Mississippi Valley.  A northerly breeze brought cold air into the mid Hudson Valley.  Although the air was not calm, the temperature of the air mass was low enough for a freeze to occur in western Dutchess County, ending the growing season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On October 27...a freeze officially ended the growing season in western Dutchess County as the temperature dropped to 31 degrees at the airport in Poughkeepsie.

A mixture of snow, sleet, and freezing began during the evening hours of the 15th and quickly changed to freezing rain.  The freezing rain persisted for 6 to 8 hours before it changed to rain.A trained spotter in Middletown reported 0.50 inches of ice accumulation with roadways glazed over.  Many traffic accidents occurred.

A tree fell on a moving car on Jackie Robinson Parkway in Flushing Queens.  Injuries were unknown.  A tree fell on a person in Clove Lakes Park, about 1 mile north of Todt Hill in Staten Island.  Injury was unknown.

A vigorous upper level disturbance tracked across the Great Lakes during the evening hours of December 8th with snows breaking out across the area during the early morning hours on the 9th.  A secondary low developed off the Mid Atlantic coast and was located near the northern Delmarva region at 7 am.  The snow was the heaviest through mid morning and ended by early afternoon.  The low rapidly deepened as it tracked to the northeast of the area.Snowfall amounts ranged from 6 inches in Manhattan to just over a foot in northern portions of the Lower Hudson Valley.Selected snowfall amounts include:5.8 inches at the Central Park Zoo6.0 inches in the Bronx8.0 inches at White Plains8.5 inches at Nyack10.0 inches at Newburgh12.0 inches at Port Jervis13.0 inches at Hamptonburgh

An area of weak low pressure moved through the eastern Great Lakes Friday morning, December 9th. Meanwhile, a weak low pressure system developed off of Delaware early on the 9th of December and moved northeast into the Gulf of Maine by late in the day. Snow overspread the area during the early morning. The precipitation tapered off to snow showers during the afternoon into the evening. General snowfall was 3 to 6 inches.

With wet antecedent conditions and many trees still with nearly full leaf canopies, strong northeast winds and heavy rain caused by a coastal storm downed many trees across the region.  Peak wind gusts were between 40 and 50 mph.The NYC Parks Department reported 169 downed trees in Queens, 98 downed trees in Brooklyn, 27 downed trees in Staten Island, 22 downed trees in Manhattan, and 19 downed trees in the Bronx.  The NYC Office of Emergency Management logged 335 telephone calls in response to downed trees, downed limbs, and hanging limbs across the 5 boroughs of New York City.A trained spotter in Suffolk County reported several downed tree limbs along with a utility pole snapped in half just west of the Route 25A and Edwards Avenue intersection in Baiting Hollow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed numerous trees and wires.  Several structures had roof damage including the roof of a barn in Remsen.  In Rome the storm took down about 2 dozen trees, some wires, and 6 power poles.  Golf Course Road in Rome was closed due to trees, wires, and poles across the road.  One tree fell on a car parked in a driveway on the same road.  A car was crushed on Pine Street by an oak tree that fell.  A tree fell on a house on Kent Street in Rome.  Two infants were injured by shattered glass caused by the tree.  On Ford Street, a tree fell on a moving car trapping the driver.  On West Thomas Street, a large tree crushed a boat in a driveway.  Other areas affected were Cleveland, Camden, Verona, Westmoreland, Vienna, Western, and Florence.
The broadcast media reports that a tree was snapped in half in Guilderland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed off the southeast U.S. coast Monday morning, December 5th.  This low gradually intensified as it moved northeast and passed southeast of Long Island Monday night. Snow developed during Monday afternoon.  Periods of heavy snow occurred during Monday night.Here are selected snowfall amounts for Eastern Suffolk County:5.5 inches was measured at Moriches and Patchogue Village6.0 inches was measured at Bridgehampton and East Quogue.

A strong high pressure system moved very slowly east to southeast across Northern New England while a low pressure trough extended northwest from a subtropical low near Bermuda.  The trough extended from south of Long Island across North Central Pennsylvania.  Waves of low pressure developed and moved northwest along this boundary.This system produced northeast gales from October 12th through the 14th with waves up to 15 feet that broke on shore.  Moderate to severe beach erosion occurred along with widespread moderate tidal flooding.  Many sections of Long Island's south shore and east end beaches were washed out.  Widespread significant damage occurred to dunes and bluffs.  Severe erosion was reported at Robert Moses State Park, Captree State Park, Hecksher State Park, and along the Montauk shores.Tidal flooding occurred starting with the October 12th afternoon high tide cycle and lasted in some areas until the afternoon high tide cycle on October 14th.Minor tidal flooding was indicated by the USGS Tidal Gage at Rockaway Inlet near Floyd Bennet Field, which measured a water level around 5.8 feet above NGVD.Moderate tidal flooding occurred across the inland bays and the Atlantic shores of Nassau County.  Here are selected water level measurements from USGS tidal gages:The East Rockaway Inlet Tidal Gage at Atlantic Beach measured a water level around 5.5 feet above NGVD, which indicated moderate tidal flooding.The Jamaica Bay Tidal Gage at Inwood measured a water level of 5.9 feet above NGVD, which indicated minor tidal flooding.  In Woodmere, water accumulated up to 2 feet in the streets.The Reynolds Channel Tidal Gage at Point Lookout measured a water level near 5.5 feet above NGVD, which indicated moderate tidal flooding.The Hudson Bay Tidal Gage at Freeport measured a water level around 5.2 feet above NGVD, which indicated moderate tidal flooding.  In Freeport, water rose over the bulk head and flooded at least 3 buildings.  Because of tidal piling, subsequent high tides through October 14th resulted in the accumulation of 1 to 3 feet of water in the streets.Moderate tidal flooding occurred across the inland bays and Atlantic facing shores of Suffolk County.The USGS Great South Bay tidal gage at Lindenhurst measured a water level of around 4.2 feet above NGVD, which indicated moderate tidal flooding.  Because of tidal piling, subsequent high tides through October 14th resulted in the accumulation of 1 to 3 feet of water in the streets from Amityville east to Babylon, including Lindenhurst.

As a strong low pressure system rapidly intensified and moved east of the area, high winds developed in its' wake.  The Automated Surface Observing Systems at Westhampton Beach and Montauk Point measured peak wind gusts to 56 mph at 11:51 am and to 54 mph at 12:43 pm.  The weather station at East Hampton measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.A trained spotter measured wind gusts from 61 to 70 mph in Wading River from 11:49 am until 1:13 pm.Law enforcement reported downed trees and power lines in Southold.Amateur radio operators reported downed trees and power lines with broken windows in East Hampton.A trained spotter reported many downed large branches and trees in Sag Harbor.

A trained spotter reported that multiple trees and power lines were brought down across roadways in Cedarville, Winfield, Frankfort Hill and Fairfield in southern Herkimer County from heavy wet snow.  Snow depth on the ground was estimated to be 5 inches in the town of Fairfield.  Power outages were reported north of Little Falls between 6 PM and 8 PM EST on October 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the period October 25 through 26, an extratropical cyclone drew in warm moist tropical air from the remnant of hurricane Wilma to become a powerful storm centered off the southern New England coast.  The storm brought heavy rain and snow to eastern New York.  Heavy snow was confined mostly to elevations above about 15 hundred feet in the southern Adirondack and Catskill mountains.  Minor flooding occurred as some small streams and main stem rivers overflowed their banks.  The flooding occurred southward from the Mohawk River where 1.5 to 3 inches of rainfall accumulated during the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement personnel reported that trees and wires were down on Corbin Hill Road in the town of Charleston due to heavy wet snow.  However, the snow depth on the ground was estimated to be only 3 inches.
On October 21...a freeze officially ended the growing season in western Ulster County as the temperature dropped to 31 degrees at Slide Mountain.
On October 21...a freeze officially ended the growing season in northern Fulton County as the temperature dropped to 29 degrees at Peck Lake.
On October 21...a freeze officially ended the growing season in southeast Warren County as the temperature dropped to 29 degrees at the airport in Glens Falls and 30 degrees at Glens Falls Farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The core of a deep-layer cold air mass moved through eastern Canada on Oct 20 and 21.  A surface ridge of high pressure extended from the center of an anticyclone over the Great Lakes into eastern New York.  A clear sky allowed optimal overnight cooling, and temperatures fell to freezing or below freezing in much of eastern New York, ending the growing season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On October 21...a freeze officially ended the growing season in southern Washington County as the temperature dropped to 30 degrees at Buskirk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong west pressure gradient developed as an intensifying low pressure system moved north across Northern New England as a ridge of high pressure extended south across the Western Great Lakes.  With very wet antecedent conditions and most trees with full leaf canopies, many trees were downed in saturated ground as peak west winds gusted from 36 to 46 mph.An off duty NWS employee reported a tree down on a house in Hampton Bays.  The NYC Office of Emergency Management reported trees down at many locations throughout Staten Island, Brooklyn, and Queens.  They also reported that a window on the 17th floor at Seventh Avenue and Forty Ninth Street in Manhattan was blown out as glass shattered in the streets.  A tree fell on a moving car on Jackie Robinson Parkway in Flushing Queens.  Injuries were unknown.  A tree fell on a person in Clove Lakes Park, about 1 mile north of Todt Hill in Staten Island.  Injury was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved slowly east, a wave of low pressure developed along the Southeast U.S. Coast and moved slowly north as a large area of high pressure off the New England Coast remained nearly stationary.  Abundant moisture from the remnants of tropical system Tammy over the Southeast also interacted with and converged north along this front.Rain moved north and developed across the region during Friday afternoon, October 7th.  As the wave of low pressure moved north along the front, periods of heavy rain spread north across the region Friday night through Saturday, October 8th.  Rain ended from west to east during Sunday, October 9th as the low moved northeast of the region.The heaviest rain fell north of New York City across the Lower Hudson Valley.  Heavy rain resulted in significant flooding on some rivers, most small brooks and streams, and throughout urban areas in low lying and poor drainage areas.  Rainfall amounts ranged from 1.10 inch at Westhampton Beach in Suffolk County to 12.05 inches at New Windsor in Orange County.Here are selected rainfall amounts in hundredths of an inch for:Orange County - from 5.58 inches at Gardnerville to 12.05 inches at New Windsor.Rockland County - 8.63 inches was measured at Pomona.Westchester County - from 5.25 inches at Westchester County Airport in White Plains to 6.28 inches at Yorktown Heights.Bronx County - 4.16 inches was measured at Parkchester.New York County - 4.50 inches was measured at Central Park.Kings County - from 2.20 inches at Avenue V in Brooklyn to 2.70 inches at Sheepshead Bay.Queens County - from 1.84 inches at John F. Kennedy Airport to 4.01 inches at LaGuardia Airport.Nassau County - from 1.69 inches at Cedar Creek in Wantagh to 3.00 inches at Plainview.Suffolk County - from 1.10 inches at Westhampton Beach to 3.14 inches at Centerport.Maximum hourly rainfall rates ranged from 0.61 at Montgomery Airport to 0.89 at Central Park.Trained spotters reported widespread flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas.  Spotters reported flash flooding of many roads throughout Orange County.  A state of emergency was declared for Highland Town, where up to 4 feet of water covered Routes 293 and 9W.  Significant flooding was reported in Cold Springs (Putnam County), in Nanuet along Interstate-87 (Rockland County) and in Yorktown (Westchester County).The Mahwah River at Suffern was above its' flood stage of 4.0 feet from 7:30 pm EDT on October 8th through 8:45 pm EDT on October 9th.  The river crested at 7.11 feet at 11:30 pm EDT on October 8th.  Other rivers and streams, such as the Wallkill River at Gardiner, experienced significant within bank rises.  Spotters from Orange County reported that many small streams and creeks overflowed their banks.  The Westchester County Office of Emergency Management reported significant flooding and road closures along the Saw Mill River and adjacent parkway.

A strong high pressure system moved very slowly east to southeast across Northern New England while a low pressure trough extended northwest from a subtropical low near Bermuda.  The trough extended from south of Long Island across North Central Pennsylvania.  Waves of low pressure that formed and moved northwest along this boundary periodically enhanced rainfall rates north of the boundary.Periods of heavy rain accompanied by strong northeast winds from October 11th through the 14th caused widespread urban and small stream flooding across the local area.The Mahwah River at Suffern was above its' flood stage of 4.0 feet from around 5 pm EDT on October 12th until 4:15 pm EDT on October 15th.  It crested at 6.15 feet at 11:45 pm on October 12th.Excessive rainfall occurred from October 11th through the 14th.  The heaviest rain fell across Long Island.  Nine daily rainfall records were set at 4 locations:At Central Park, new daily rainfall records of 4.26 inches and 2.75 inches were set on October 12th and 13th.At LaGuardia Airport, new daily rainfall records of 4.39 inches and 1.63 inches were set on October 12th and 13th.At JFK Airport, new daily rainfall records of 3.06 inches, 1.93 inches, and 4.66 inches were set on October 12th, 13th, and 14th.At Islip Airport, new daily rainfall records of 3.30 inches and 5.38 inches were set on October 13th and 14th.Here are selected rainfall amounts in hundredths of an inch for:Orange County - from 4.74 inches at Westtown to 10.30 inches at New Windsor.Westchester County - 7.43 inches was measured at Westchester County Airport in White Plains.New York County - 8.84 inches was measured at Central Park.Kings County - from 7.92 inches at Midwood to 8.05 inches at Sheepshead Bay.Queens County - from 7.70 inches at LaGuardia Airport to 11.18 inches at JFK Airport.Nassau County - from 10.01 inches at Hicksville to 15.97 inches at Lynbrook.Suffolk County - from 10.39 inches at Islip Airport to 17.92 inches at Wading River.

Periods of heavy rain continued throughout the day.  Bands of torrential rain produced widespread and significant flash flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas, including streets, basements, roofs, and ceilings, as well as small creeks throughout Nassau and Suffolk Counties.  Trained spotters reported up to 6 feet of water in basements throughout Massapequa.  They reported serious flooding along Ocean Parkway in Brooklyn, where cars were submerged, and the Grand Central Parkway in Queens.  Several trees and power poles were leaning from soggy ground.  Preliminary damage cost estimates were $22 million for Long Island.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: On October 21...a freeze officially ended the growing season in northern Saratoga County as the temperature dropped to 30 degrees at Saratoga Springs and 31 degrees at Conklingville Dam.

Periods of heavy rain caused flooding of low lying and poor drainage areas, including many streets.  An emergency manager reported cars submerged on the Grand Central Parkway.  The broadcast media reported street flooding in Manhattan along the West Side Highway and Hudson Parkway and in Brooklyn along the Belt Parkway.  Trained spotters reported flooding of many area local streets.

An early season snowfall hit parts of upstate New York on October 25th, 2005. Low pressure developed over Ohio and tracked to off the New Jersey Coast where it intensified into a major early season winter storm. Snow fell mainly over the higher elevations of central New York and the Catskills, with mainly rain in the valleys. The rain caused minor flood problems in low-lying areas near Waverly along the Susquehanna River. The hardest hits areas were from the higher terrain of southern and eastern Broome County, north and east through the higher terrain of Chenango, western Delaware, eastern Cortland, southern Madison, Otsego and southern Onondaga counties. Between 6 and 12 inches of heavy wet snow fell above 1400 feet elevation in these areas. Highest amounts were found in southern Madison County and in Chenango County at 1800 feet where more than 12 inches of wet snow fell. Between 2 and 6 inches of snow fell between 1000 and 1400 feet in these areas. The snow came down very hard from the late morning into the early and mid afternoon hours on the 25th. The snow continued heavy at times until the mid to late evening hours on the 25th. The snow rapidly accumulated on roads and caused major traffic problems along interstates 81, 88 and route 17 in southern New York and in the Binghamton area. In addition, the heavy wet snow brought down trees and power lines in these areas. Over 10,000 customers were without power in Broome and Chenango Counties alone.  Across the Central Southern Tier, and the Finger Lakes region, the valleys saw rain with the higher terrain, above 1600 feet, seeing only a few inches of snow. In the Catskills, the higher terrain above 1800 feet saw up to 6 inches of snow. The Syracuse and Utica areas saw little snowfall, with the Southern Tug Hill Plateau reporting up to a few inches.
Thunderstorms with torrential downpours were caused by the interaction of offshore Tropical Storm Ophelia and a cold front to the west of New York City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rains caused flash flooding of Streets.
Trees were blown down in Glens Falls.
Hundreds of trees and power poles were blown down.  Roads were impassable. Damage is attributable to both severe thunderstorms and non thunderstorm wind.
Trees were blown down in Speculator.
Trees and power lines were blown down at Stratford.
Trees and power lines were blown down in Gloversville.
Trees were blown down in Old Forge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large ocean storm system moved north, passing east of New England on Tuesday, October 25th and Wednesday, October 26th. Rain on the 25th of October changed to snow by early afternoon in the higher terrain. This heavy wet snow was accompanied by gusty winds. With foliage still on the trees, the weight of the snow easily took many trees and tree limbs down with extensive power outages. During the evening, it was clearly evident that the impact of the storm was being felt with 3 to 6 inches of heavy wet snow. Total snow accumulations in this area were 5 to 10 inches with lesser amounts in some sheltered valleys. Specifically, Ellenburg Depot (western Clinton county) received 10 inches, while the Franklin county towns of Whippleville received 9 inches, and Tupper Lake 5 inches. In western Essex county, Lake Placid received 6 1/2 inches of heavy wet snow.

On October 12 and 13, a nearly stationary low pressure system off the Virginia coast drew moist Atlantic air into southeastern New York.  Rainfall over this period was between an inch and a half and 3 inches over the bulk of southeastern New York.  With the ground soaked and the rivers high from heavy rainfall a few days earlier, rainfall from this storm was enough to produce flooding on many waterways in the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gage at Roundout Reservoir on Roundout Creek had a flood crest of 840.45 feet on Oct 15, at 10:00 AM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Near daybreak on September 29, an intensifying low pressure was north of Lake Ontario.  A sharp cold front trailed southward along the west side of the Appalachain Mountains.  A strong pressure gradient was across New York State and western New England.  The cold front moved quickly through eastern New York and western New England during the afternoon.  A strong south wind occurred over an extensive portion of eastern New York prior to the arrival of the cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the vicinity of Hudson and Kinderhook by strong winds.

Arctic air moved in during December 6th behind a departing coastal low pressure system.  This cold air created lake effect snow east of lake Ontario.  A band of heavy lake effect snow moved into northern Oneida County around 6 PM on December 6th.  The band was stationary over central and northern Oneida County through the night before shifting slightly south into northern Onondaga and northern Madison Counties around 5 AM.  The band was stationary for a few hours before being pushed further south by a cold front early in the afternoon.  Snowfall rates were 1 to 3 inches an hour.  Like a previous lake effect event on the 2nd to the 3rd, west winds of 15 to 20 mph caused blowing and drifting snow, and whiteout conditions.  Snowfall amounts were 8 to 18 inches in northern Oneida County, and 6 to 12 inches in northern Onondaga, northern Madison, and southern Oneida Counties.

Surface low pressure moving up the east coast brought copious moisture north ahead of it.  Snow started the afternoon of the 15th, then  continued into the morning of the 16th before ending.  The snow was mixed with sleet and freezing rain during the morning of the 16th.  Snowfall amounts were 4 to 10 inches.  This was a heavy wet snow with around 1 inch of liquid water in it.  The mixed precipitation on the roads created many vehicular accidents.

A large area of high pressure centered over southeast Canada brought arctic cold air to the area.  Morning low temperatures were almost all below zero, with most between 5 below and 15 below zero.  Dozens of airports and cooperative stations broke the record low temperature for the day.  Calm to light winds and snow cover helped the temperatures get as cold as they did.  These were the coldest temperatures of the season up to this point.

Lake snows began to develop late in the evening of the 18th and intensified overnight. The snows remained nearly stationary until the afternoon hours. Over two feet of snow fell over the Tug Hill. Snowfall totals included: North Osceola 28"; Pulaski 22"; Oswego 14"; and Highmarket 13".

Low pressure brought a mix of wintry precipitation to parts of the Finger Lakes region during the overnight. Freezing rain accumulated up to a quarter inch of ice before the precipitation changed to snow. Five to seven inches of snow then fell on top of the ice, before tapering off during the morning hours.

Lake effect snows developed to the southeast of Lakes Erie and Ontario. The two-day event brought the bulk of snow off Lake Erie on the 2nd and off Lake Ontario on the 3rd. Snowfall amounts were impressive and were focused well inland rather than along the lake shores. Specific snowfall reports included: off Lake Erie 18" at Sherman and Jamestown; 13" at Cherry Hill; 9" at Ellicottville; and 8" at Friendship; off Lake Ontario 20" at West Monroe and Constantia; 10" at Cato and 7" at Barnes Corners.

An upper trough cross the Great Lakes reinforcing the arctic air over the region. Lake effect snows fell downwind of Lakes Erie and Ontario. Off Lake Erie, the activity focused on northern Chautauqua, northern Cattaraugus, southern Erie and southern Wyoming counties. Over 20 inches fell in a narrow band along the three county line. Reports from spotters included: Perrysburg 29"; Arcade 24"; Chaffee 22"; South Dayton 15"; Dunkirk 13"; Colden 8"; and Ellicottville 7". Off Lake Ontario, the lake snows began over central Jefferson and northern Lewis counties settling south over the Tug Hill and eventually across southern Oswego county. Reported snowfall totals included: Mexico 17"; Highmarket 16"; Constantia 14" and Oswego 12".

The snow moved into the area late in the evening of the 8th.  Snowfall amounts were mostly from 6 to 10 inches.  In southern Sullivan County amounts were higher, from 9 to 12 inches.
